heading: MODULO QUARTO
card_summary: Chunk 109 belongs to the "Tono Ottavo" of the Terzo Cantico and develops Zorzi's architectonics of the heavenly Jerusalem through three concentus. The first establishes that the supreme Architect (drawing on Wisdom 7:21 and Apocalypse 21) built three dwelling-places — celestial kingdom, earthly world, and the Church — whose harmony allows each to be understood from the others. The second concentus meditates on the "glue" (glutinum) that binds creation: after surveying physical adhesives from mortar to animal glues and bodily connective tissue, Zorzi argues that divine force alone does not merely connect (connectere) but fully unites (unire), and that this force is the divine Name itself, cited from Psalm 8, 2 Chronicles, Deuteronomy, Jeremiah, and Revelation. The third concentus turns to kabbalistic gematria to explain why the apostles' names are inscribed in the foundations and angelic names on the gates of the heavenly city: Zorzi provides detailed Hebrew numerical analysis of Abram/Abraham (243/248, corresponding to the bones of the human body and the affirmative commandments), Sara/Sarai, and the names Israel, Isaac, John, and Jesus, concluding that the divine Name pervades and deifies the entire edifice.
essay:
# MODULO QUARTO (pp. 2519–2532): The Architect, the Glue, and the Names

## Overview

This chunk constitutes the "Quarto Modulo" of the Tono Ottavo within the Terzo Cantico. It is organised in three *concentus* addressing: (1) the identity of the builder of the heavenly city; (2) the binding force — physical, natural, and divine — that holds the cosmic edifice together; (3) the kabbalistic rationale for the inscription of apostolic, tribal, and angelic names on the foundations and gates of the New Jerusalem. The section is a sustained exercise in Christian Kabbalah, weaving Hebrew gematria, angelology, and architectural metaphor into Zorzi's broader cosmological project of demonstrating the harmonic unity of creation.

---

## 1. The Three Dwellings and Their Architect

Zorzi opens by identifying the builder of the heavenly Jerusalem through Revelation 21:2 ("Vidi la città santa di Gerusalemme discendere dal cielo, preparata da Dio, come una sposa adorna per il suo sposo"), naming the architect with the Greek term *calotechnius* — "buon architetto, artefice e decoratore" — a hapax-style coinage that carries resonances of both *kalos* (beautiful) and *technê* (craft), underscoring the aesthetic as well as constructive dimensions of divine creation.

The supreme Architect is said to have prepared a *dimora triplice* for humankind:

1. The celestial kingdom containing the *dimore dei beati*
2. The earthly world (*regno mondano*) as the domain of pilgrimage, whose elegant disposition allows us to conjecture the beauty of the eternal kingdom
3. The intermediate spiritual dwelling — the Church — through whose perimeter we must pass to ascend to the divine dwelling

The three buildings share a *relazione simbolica* because they were fashioned by the same Artificer:

> «Questi tre edifici sono simili tra loro poiché furono costruiti dal medesimo Artefice e ciascuno di essi, secondo le proprie capacità, ne reca l'immagine.»

<cite>p. 2351 (Italian translation)</cite>

The theological source is Wisdom 7:21 ("Omnium est enim artifex, omnium habens virtutem, omnia prospiciens"), cited in the Latin text as the definitive characterisation of the divine builder.

---

## 2. The Glutinum — Hierarchy of Binding Forces

The second *concentus* is philosophically the richest. Zorzi catalogues the means by which composite things are held together, ascending from the lowest (material art) to the highest (divine mystery):

- **Art**: mortar (*lithocolla*), lime, iron or bronze cramps, lead; carpenter's glues (casein, fish glue, bull-hide glue, the last considered *perfectissima*)
- **Nature**: nerves, flesh, skin; tree-grafting; oysters clinging to rock; human bodies petrified and fused with stone into a single *corpus homogeneum*
- **Divine power**: transcends connection (*connectere*) and achieves true union (*unire*)

> «Tuttavia, poiché la potenza divina è di gran lunga superiore a tutte le altre, essa non si limita a connettere ma è in grado di unire.»

<cite>p. 2353 (Italian translation)</cite>

Zorzi argues that this supreme binding force is the divine Name itself. He adduces a dense chain of scriptural proof-texts:

- **Psalm 8:1** ("Signore nostro Dio, com'è mirabile il tuo nome su tutta la terra") — the Name operates on both the lower and the upper earth simultaneously
- **2 Chronicles (Paralipomenon)**: "Ho scelto Gerusalemme, perché il mio nome dimori in essa" — the Jerusalem Temple is a typological image of the heavenly city united by the Name
- **Deuteronomy**: after the commandment on charity binding priests, Levites, servants, and pilgrims, the Lord adds "Perché il mio nome dimori là"
- **Jeremiah**: "Tu sei in mezzo a noi, Signore, e il tuo nome è invocato sopra di noi"
- **Genesis 4:26**: the restoration of Abel's line in Seth and Enos — "Allora si incominciò a invocare il nome del Signore" — where Zorzi corrects the received Latin text (*mendosi textus*) to read an impersonal invocation, interpreting the Name as *nexus, glutinum, et vinculum* of the renewed human family

The Name is written on the elect progressively: first in baptism (the theological *character* of the scholastics), then again in triumph when the victor becomes a *columna* in the Temple of God, receiving the name of God, the name of the New Jerusalem, and the *nomen novum* — Jesus (Revelation 3:12). Zorzi glosses Augustine's formula "Visio est tota merces" to identify the beatific vision with the full, permanent inscription of the Name on the blessed.

---

## 3. Kabbalistic Gematria of the Divine Names

The third *concentus* provides the most technically elaborate material. Zorzi asks why apostolic and tribal names are on the foundations while angelic names are on the gates. His answer involves both a theology of the Name's *latent power* (*latens virtus*) and an extended kabbalistic exercise.

### The Name's Hidden Power and Onomastic Theology

The inscription refers not to mere letters but to the latent power contained in names, by which God knows the elect: "Ti conosco per nome." Zorzi notes that only a few names explicitly given by God are recorded in Scripture: Abraham, Isaac, Israel, Jedidiah, John, Jesus.

### Abram → Abraham: Gematria of the Letter He (ה)

The paradigm case is the addition of ה (*he*, numerical value 5) to Abram's name:

> «Abramo, infatti, si chiamava prima Abram ma, per comando di Dio (come si legge nella Genesi) fu chiamato Abramo, aggiungendo al suo nome la lettera (ה), che ha valore numerico cinque.»

<cite>p. 2357 (Italian translation)</cite>

- **Abram** (אברם) = 243
- **Abraham** (אברהם) = 248

The value 248 equals the number of bones in the human body and the number of positive commandments (*praecepta affirmativa*) in Jewish tradition. The divine command "Cammina alla mia presenza e sii integro" (Genesis 17:1) is thus encoded in the name itself: integrity (*perfectus*) means observing all positive commandments, symbolised by all the bones.

Zorzi then performs a further operation: the letter *he* (5) was taken *from* Sarah's name:

- **Sarai** (שרי) = 510
- **Sara** (שרה) = 505

What was removed from the woman was added to the man (in contrast to the primal event where the rib was taken from Adam to form Eve). This generates a moral-cosmological typology: Adam's fall was grounded in this earlier privation; Abraham's perfection in the restitution. The comparison is left deliberately restrained ("Non vogliamo qui spingerci oltre i confini del lecito").

### Isaac, Israel, John, Jesus

Zorzi proceeds more briefly through further onomastic examples:

- **Isaac**: named by Abraham in allusion to Sarah's laughter at the angelic oracle, signifying joy (*gaudium*) as opposed to Ishmael's burden (*onus*)
- **Israel**: received from the wrestling angel, interpreted as "principe, o erede, di Dio" (*princeps vel haeres Dei*)
- **John**: named by an angel so that the meaning "grazia di Dio" would befit the precursor of the dispensation of grace
- **Jesus**: treated as already discussed elsewhere; described as the *nomen novum* that contains the ancient names (*nomina antiqua*) within itself

### Apostles on the Foundations, Angels on the Gates

The structural argument: apostles are inscribed on the foundations because they *founded* the Church in the twelve regions of the world upon the unique universal foundation, Christ Jesus (1 Corinthians 3; Ephesians 2:20: "Superaedificati supra fundamentum apostolorum, et prophetarum").

Angels are inscribed on the gates because their function is to *introduce* the elect into the royal court. Zorzi cites Exodus 23:20 ("Ecco, io mando il mio angelo perché ti preceda") and Luke 16 (the angels carrying Lazarus to the place of salvation). The angelic hierarchy at the gates is then mapped with precision:

- Seven princes before the throne of God
- Four angels assigned to harm earth and sea from the four cardinal points
- Seventy-two angels over seventy-two languages
- Twelve angels over the twelve zodiacal signs (the "twelve gates of heaven")

Isaiah 62:6 ("Sulle tue mura, Gerusalemme, ho posto dei custodi") confirms angelic custodianship of the walls. Malachi 2:7 equates priests with angels ("angelus domini exercituum"), allowing Zorzi to read the apostolic function as simultaneously angelic: the Twelve are both the foundations and the angelic gatekeepers, since they exercise the "chiavi evangeliche."

The section closes with an architectural synthesis:

> «Il nome di Dio, però, si trova in tutto l'edificio, e sulla fronte di ciascuno degli ospiti, con la sua azione di legame, vincolo di unione, consolidamento, vivificazione e deificazione dell'intera struttura.»

<cite>p. 2363 (Italian translation)</cite>

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk advances several threads central to *De Harmonia Mundi*:

- **Harmonic analogy across levels of being**: the three dwellings (celestial, terrestrial, ecclesial) form a harmonic series derivable from one another, consistent with Zorzi's Neoplatonic emanationism
- **The Name as cosmic bond**: the kabbalistic *shem* (divine Name) functions here as the equivalent of the Neoplatonic *logos* or the Hermetic *spiritus mundi*, the dynamic force sustaining cosmic unity
- **Gematria as theological argument**: the Abram/Abraham analysis shows Zorzi's debt to Pico della Mirandola's *Conclusiones* and to Jewish kabbalistic commentaries on Genesis 17, deploying Hebrew numerology as evidence for Christian theological claims about embodiment, commandment, and perfection
- **Angelology and ecclesiology**: the mapping of angelic hierarchies onto apostolic and zodiacal structures demonstrates Zorzi's characteristic fusion of Dionysian hierarchical theology with astrological cosmology and New Testament ecclesiology
